posts.title: Agents are infrastructure now
posts.hook: AI agents stopped being a demo and started being infrastructure about 3 months ago. Most teams haven't noticed yet.
posts.body:
AI agents stopped being a demo and started being infrastructure about 3 months ago. Most teams haven't noticed yet.

Vercel shipped serverless agent deployment. Cursor and Replit baked agentic workflows into the editor. Claude Code runs multi-file refactors across entire repos.

But the real shift isn't the tools. It's the expectation.

I run a solo GTM operation. One person. Full ABM pipeline, three websites, content distribution, CRM enrichment, prospect research. Six months ago that required a team of 4-5.

The difference isn't that I'm faster at typing. It's that agents handle the orchestration layer I used to do manually. Data enrichment runs on a cron. Content gets distributed across platforms from a single source. Lead scoring updates itself.

The teams still treating AI as "a better autocomplete" are about to get lapped by solo operators and small teams who treat it as infrastructure.

This isn't about replacing people. It's about what becomes possible when one person can operate at the scale of a small team.

If you're building GTM right now and your agents aren't running autonomously on crons, you're still in the demo phase.
posts.cta: What's the first workflow you moved from manual to autonomous? Genuinely curious what people are automating first.

inspired_by.author: Guillermo Rauch
inspired_by.summary: Announces updates to Vercel AI SDK for easier deployment of AI agents with serverless infra. Demonstrates how it simplifies scaling automation pipelines for developers. Ties into broader trends in edge AI infrastructure.

remixes.label: infrastructure-debt-angle
remixes.hook: The companies building AI agents as side projects will be rewriting them as core infrastructure within 6 months. I know because I already did.
remixes.body:
The companies building AI agents as side projects will be rewriting them as core infrastructure within 6 months. I know because I already did.

Six months ago my agents were scripts. Clever automations duct-taped to cron jobs. They worked, but they were fragile. One API change and three workflows broke simultaneously.

So I rebuilt them as infrastructure. Proper error handling. Retry logic. Observability. The same boring stuff you'd build for any production system.

The difference was immediate. My content pipeline stopped breaking at 2am. My prospecting enrichment stopped silently failing. My daily reporting actually ran daily.

Here's what changed in practice:

Before: agent breaks, I find out when output stops appearing.
After: agent breaks, I get a log entry, it retries, and I find out in the morning brief.

Before: adding a new workflow meant a new script and a prayer.
After: adding a new workflow means plugging into existing orchestration.

The real cost of treating agents as experiments isn't that they fail. It's that you build around their unreliability instead of fixing it. You add manual checks. You eyeball outputs. You become the error handler.

Infrastructure means you trust it enough to stop watching it. That's the bar. Most agent implementations aren't there yet.
remixes.cta: What's the first agent workflow you promoted from 'experiment' to 'infrastructure'? What broke that forced the upgrade?

remixes.label: expectation-shift-narrative
remixes.hook: My clients used to ask 'can AI do this?' Now they ask 'why isn't AI already doing this?' That expectation shift happened in about 90 days.
remixes.body:
My clients used to ask 'can AI do this?' Now they ask 'why isn't AI already doing this?' That expectation shift happened in about 90 days.

I noticed it first in discovery calls. A founder asked me why his outbound sequences weren't being personalized by AI automatically. Not whether they could be. Why they weren't already.

That's an infrastructure expectation. The same way nobody asks 'can we deploy to the cloud?' anymore. It's assumed.

Agents crossed that line quietly. No single announcement. No product launch that flipped the switch. It was cumulative.

Vercel made agent deployment as simple as deploying a Next.js app. Claude Code started doing multi-file refactors that previously took a senior engineer half a day. Cursor turned the IDE into a conversation.

The tooling matured faster than the organizational awareness. Which means there's a gap right now between what's possible and what most teams are doing.

I run my entire GTM operation solo. Content creation, prospecting, enrichment, outreach sequencing, reporting. Not because I'm exceptional. Because the infrastructure exists and I bothered to wire it up.

The window where this feels like a competitive advantage is closing. Within a year, this will just be how things work. The question is whether you're building on that foundation now or retrofitting later.
remixes.cta: When did the expectation shift hit your team? The moment someone assumed AI should already be handling something it wasn't?

posts.title: GTM engineering isn't new
posts.hook: Everyone's calling "GTM engineering" a new discipline. It's not new. It's what happens when SDRs learn to code.
posts.body:
Everyone's calling "GTM engineering" a new discipline. It's not new. It's what happens when SDRs learn to code.

I spent years building buying committees manually in Salesforce. 200+ emails a day from primary domains with no warmup. Learning which signals mattered by watching what actually converted.

That grind taught me something no engineering bootcamp could. The data model for revenue isn't theoretical. It's built from thousands of reps seeing what works.

Now I build Clay tables that replicate what I used to do by hand. Enrichment waterfall that checks 4 sources before giving up on a phone number. Scoring models based on actual buying patterns, not demographic guesses.

The engineers building GTM tools without sales floor experience keep making the same mistake. They optimize for data completeness instead of signal quality. They build dashboards nobody checks. They automate sequences that sound like robots wrote them... because robots did.

The best GTM engineers I know all share one thing. They've done the manual work. They know which steps actually matter because they've felt the pain of doing them 500 times.

SDRs make the best GTM engineers. Not because they're better coders. Because they know what to build.
posts.cta: If you came from sales into building, what's the one thing your engineering peers consistently miss about the GTM workflow?

inspired_by.author: Sami Rahman
inspired_by.summary: Discusses building scalable GTM engines by integrating engineering principles into go-to-market strategies. Emphasizes data-driven experimentation and automation to accelerate revenue growth. Shares a framework for aligning product, sales, and marketing teams.

remixes.label: sales-floor-advantage
remixes.hook: The best GTM engineers I know didn't come from CS programs. They came from sales floors where they got tired of doing the same thing 200 times a day.
remixes.body:
The best GTM engineers I know didn't come from CS programs. They came from sales floors where they got tired of doing the same thing 200 times a day.

There's a specific kind of knowledge you only get from manually working a pipeline. You learn which signals actually predict a closed deal versus which ones look good in a dashboard. You learn that job title means almost nothing compared to buying behavior. You learn that the CRM data model everyone designed in a conference room doesn't match how deals actually move.

I built buying committees by hand in Salesforce for years. 200+ emails a day from primary domains. No warmup tools, no sequencing platforms. Just volume and pattern recognition.

That grind gave me something I couldn't have learned any other way. I know what data matters because I watched what actually converted. Not what the data team hypothesized would convert. What actually did.

When I started automating those workflows, I wasn't guessing which signals to prioritize. I'd already run the experiment manually, thousands of times.

That's the gap most pure engineers have when they build GTM tooling. They optimize for data completeness when they should optimize for signal quality. They build dashboards that track everything and surface nothing.

The sales floor teaches you what to ignore. That's the real skill.
remixes.cta: What's something you learned on the sales floor that you've never seen captured in a GTM tool? The tribal knowledge that nobody documents.

remixes.label: discipline-not-role
remixes.hook: GTM engineering isn't a job title. It's what happens when someone refuses to accept that their tools don't talk to each other.
remixes.body:
GTM engineering isn't a job title. It's what happens when someone refuses to accept that their tools don't talk to each other.

I didn't set out to become a GTM engineer. I was an SDR who got frustrated. The CRM didn't connect to the enrichment tool. The enrichment tool didn't connect to the sequencer. The sequencer didn't tell me what was actually working.

So I wrote scripts. Ugly ones at first. Python that pulled from one API and pushed to another. Cron jobs that ran at midnight and broke by morning.

But they worked better than the manual process. And each one I built taught me something about how revenue systems should connect but don't.

That's the origin story for most GTM engineers I know. Nobody woke up and decided to become one. They just kept solving integration problems until the title caught up.

The discipline existed long before the label. Every ops person who wrote a Zapier flow to fix broken data handoffs was doing GTM engineering. Every SDR who built a Google Sheet formula to score leads was doing it. Every marketer who hacked together UTM tracking because the attribution tool was lying was doing it.

What changed is that the tools got powerful enough that these workarounds became real systems. And the people who built them became engineers, whether or not they had the degree.
remixes.cta: When did you first realize you were doing GTM engineering before the term existed? What was the problem that forced you to start building?

Everyone's calling "GTM engineering" a new discipline. It's not new. It's what happens when SDRs learn to code.

Here's the context I'd share with anyone entering this space. The skills that matter most in GTM engineering aren't technical. They're experiential.

I spent years building buying committees manually in Salesforce. 200+ emails a day from primary domains with no warmup. Learning which signals mattered by watching what actually converted.

That grind taught me something no engineering bootcamp could. The data model for revenue isn't the one in your CRM schema. It's the one that maps to how humans actually buy.

My recommendation for anyone building GTM systems: start with the manual workflow first. Do it by hand for long enough to understand which steps actually matter. Then automate with conviction instead of guessing.

The engineers who skip this step build beautiful systems that optimize for the wrong signals. They track every data point and surface none of the ones that predict conversion.

If you're hiring for this role, look for the person who's done both. The SDR who learned Python, not the engineer who read a book about sales. The pattern recognition from manual work is the foundation everything else gets built on.

Everyone's calling "GTM engineering" a new discipline. It's not new. And the hype around it is creating a problem.

People are skipping the hard part. They're learning APIs and automation tools without ever having manually worked a pipeline. And it shows in what they build.

I spent years in the grind. Building buying committees by hand in Salesforce. 200+ emails a day from primary domains with no warmup. Getting rejected constantly and learning which signals actually predicted revenue versus which ones just felt important.

You cannot shortcut that education. You can't read about it in a course. You can't absorb it from a YouTube tutorial about Clay workflows.

The data model for revenue isn't in your CRM schema. It's in the pattern recognition you build from thousands of manual reps. Which industries respond to what messaging. Which titles are decision-makers versus influencers. Which signals mean "ready to buy" versus "just browsing."

Every GTM engineer I respect has scar tissue from the manual era. The ones building tools without that foundation keep making the same mistake. They automate the process they think exists instead of the one that actually works.

Stop glamorizing the engineering part. The sales floor is where the real education happens.

posts.title: Solo operator math
posts.hook: A solo developer shipped Nomad List, Remote OK, and a dozen other products. A solo GTM engineer can run an entire pipeline. The math changed and most companies haven't updated their headcount models.
posts.body:
A solo developer shipped Nomad List, Remote OK, and a dozen other products. A solo GTM engineer can run an entire pipeline. The math changed and most companies haven't updated their headcount models.

Here's what my solo stack actually looks like:

🔧 Prospecting: Clay enrichment waterfall on a cron. Pulls from Apollo, LinkedIn, company signals. Scores and routes automatically.

🔧 Outreach: Lemlist sequences with dynamic personalization pulled from enrichment. Not "Hey {firstName}" garbage. Actual research-backed angles.

🔧 CRM: Attio with automated stage progression. No manual data entry.

🔧 Content: Claude generates drafts from my voice system. I edit and approve. Distribution hits Reddit, LinkedIn, X, and blog from one source file.

🔧 Analytics: PostHog tracking the full funnel. Which content drives which pipeline.

Total team size: one.

I'm not saying everyone should go solo. I'm saying the floor for what one person can operate just dropped dramatically. The 3-person SDR team doing manual research and generic sequences is competing against solo operators running automated intelligence.

The leverage isn't in working more hours. It's in building systems that compound while you sleep.
posts.cta: No gatekeeping. If you want to see how any piece of this stack connects, ask in the comments and I'll break it down.

inspired_by.author: Pieter Levels
inspired_by.summary: Pieter shares his journey as a solo developer building and shipping multiple products like Nomad List and Remote OK without VC funding. He emphasizes the power of building in public, iterating based on user feedback, and achieving profitability through bootstrapping. His post inspires others to start small and ship fast.

remixes.label: headcount-obsolescence
remixes.hook: I replaced a 4-person GTM team with cron jobs and API calls. Not to prove a point. Because I couldn't afford the team.
remixes.body:
I replaced a 4-person GTM team with cron jobs and API calls. Not to prove a point. Because I couldn't afford the team.

When I went solo, I didn't have a philosophy about lean operations. I had a budget constraint. So I looked at every function a typical GTM team handles and asked: can I automate this well enough that it runs without me?

Prospecting: yes. Clay enrichment waterfall on a cron. Pulls signals from Apollo, LinkedIn, company data. Scores and routes automatically. Runs at midnight, results in my inbox by morning.

Outreach: yes. Lemlist sequences triggered by enrichment scores. Personalization built from signal data, not templates. Follow-ups are conditional on engagement.

Content distribution: yes. One piece of source content generates platform-specific variants for LinkedIn, X, Reddit, newsletter. Automated pipeline from draft to distribution.

Reporting: yes. Daily brief generated automatically. Pipeline health, content performance, engagement metrics. No manual dashboard checks.

What I can't automate: strategy decisions, relationship building, creative direction, and knowing when to break the system's own rules.

The ratio is roughly 85% automated, 15% human judgment. And that 15% is where all the leverage actually lives.

Most companies are running this ratio inverted. 85% human labor on tasks that should be automated. 15% strategic thinking squeezed into whatever time is left.
remixes.cta: What's your current ratio of automated versus manual GTM work? Be honest. Where are you still doing things by hand that a script could handle?

remixes.label: compound-leverage
remixes.hook: The solo operator advantage isn't cost savings. It's decision speed. When one person controls the full pipeline, iteration cycles collapse from weeks to hours.
remixes.body:
The solo operator advantage isn't cost savings. It's decision speed. When one person controls the full pipeline, iteration cycles collapse from weeks to hours.

Think about what happens when a team of four runs a GTM pipeline. SDR notices a signal pattern. Tells ops. Ops files a ticket. Engineering scopes the change. Two sprints later, the enrichment logic gets updated.

When I notice a signal pattern, I update the scoring model before lunch. Test it on the afternoon batch. See results by the next morning. Iterate again if needed.

That speed compounds. Over a month, I run 20-30 iterations on my pipeline. A team might run 2-3 in the same period. Not because the team is slow. Because coordination has overhead that solo doesn't.

. The same iteration speed applies to mistakes. I break things faster, but I also fix them faster. My system fails forward instead of failing in committee.

Levels Fyi shipped as a side project. Nomad List was one person. Remote OK was one person. The pattern in SaaS is well-documented. It's now hitting GTM operations.

The headcount model most companies use was designed for an era when execution required humans at every step. That era is ending. The math changed. The org charts haven't caught up yet.
remixes.cta: If you could collapse one coordination bottleneck in your GTM process down to a single owner, which one would have the biggest impact?

posts.title: Personalization is a data problem
posts.hook: Outbound personalization at scale isn't a copywriting problem. It's a data engineering problem.
posts.body:
Outbound personalization at scale isn't a copywriting problem. It's a data engineering problem.

Every outbound tool promises "personalization at scale." Then you look under the hood and it's pulling the prospect's job title and recent company news into a template. That's not personalization. That's mail merge with extra steps.

Real personalization requires signal layering:

✅ What tech they actually use (not what they list on their website)
✅ Whether they're hiring for roles that indicate a problem you solve
✅ What their team has published or commented on recently
✅ How their competitors are positioning
✅ What stage of growth the company is actually in based on headcount trajectory

Each of those is a data enrichment step. Each one requires a different source. And the orchestration between them matters more than any individual data point.

I build this as a Clay table with 15+ columns before a single email gets written. The enrichment waterfall runs through Apollo, LinkedIn, company site scraping, and job board analysis. By the time the sequence fires, each prospect has a research brief attached.

The email copy itself is almost trivial at that point. When you know what someone actually cares about, writing a relevant message is easy.

Stop hiring better copywriters for outbound. Start building better data pipelines.
posts.cta: What's your hardest enrichment step? The one where data quality falls apart? That's usually where the real personalization opportunity lives.

remixes.label: mail-merge-callout
remixes.hook: If your "personalized" outbound pulls the prospect's job title and a recent press release, you're not doing personalization. You're doing mail merge with better marketing.
remixes.body:
If your "personalized" outbound pulls the prospect's job title and a recent press release, you're not doing personalization. You're doing mail merge with better marketing.

I see this constantly. Teams buy a personalization tool, connect it to their CRM, and call it done. The emails go out with the prospect's name, their company's latest funding round, maybe a reference to their job title.

The prospect sees right through it. Because everyone else is pulling the same surface-level data from the same sources. When every SDR references your Series B in their opening line, it stops feeling personal and starts feeling algorithmic.

Real personalization requires depth that most enrichment tools don't provide out of the box. You need to layer signals.

What tech they actually run. Not what they say they run. What's in their job postings, their GitHub repos, their integration pages.

What problems they're actively trying to solve. Hiring patterns reveal this. If they're hiring three data engineers, they have a data problem.

Where they are in their buying cycle. A company that just signed a competitor is a different conversation than one with an expiring contract.

Each layer requires a different data source and a different enrichment approach. The work is in building the pipeline that combines them, not in writing a better email template.
remixes.cta: What enrichment layer gives you the highest signal quality? The one where the data actually changes how you write the message.

remixes.label: enrichment-pipeline-blueprint
remixes.hook: I spent 3 months building an enrichment pipeline that layers 7 data sources into a single prospect profile. The outbound reply rate tripled. Here's why the architecture matters more than the copy.
remixes.body:
I spent 3 months building an enrichment pipeline that layers 7 data sources into a single prospect profile. The outbound reply rate tripled. Here's why the architecture matters more than the copy.

Most outbound teams spend 80% of their optimization time on email copy. Subject lines. Opening hooks. CTA variations. A/B tests on whether to use the prospect's first name or their company name.

None of that matters if you're saying the wrong thing to the wrong person at the wrong time.

The reply rate jump didn't come from better writing. It came from better targeting and better context. When you know what tech stack someone runs, what problems they're hiring for, and where they are in their vendor evaluation, the email practically writes itself.

Here's how the pipeline works:

Layer 1: firmographic basics from Apollo. Company size, industry, funding.
Layer 2: technographic data from job postings and integration pages. What they actually use.
Layer 3: hiring signals. What roles they're filling tells you what problems they're solving.
Layer 4: engagement signals. Website visits, content downloads, social activity.
Layer 5: competitive intelligence. Current vendors, contract timing, switching signals.
Layer 6: organizational mapping. Who reports to whom. Who has budget authority.
Layer 7: intent synthesis. All layers combined into a single relevance score.

Each layer has its own data source, its own update frequency, and its own failure mode. Building this as a pipeline, not a one-shot enrichment, is what makes it work.
remixes.cta: Which enrichment layer do you find hardest to get reliable data for? That's usually where the biggest opportunity lives.

tones.builder:
Outbound personalization at scale isn't a copywriting problem. It's a data engineering problem.

Every outbound tool promises "personalization at scale." Then you look under the hood and it's pulling the prospect's job title and recent company news into a template. That's not personalization. That's mail merge with extra steps.

Real personalization requires signal layering:

✅ What tech they actually use (not what they list on their website)
✅ Whether they're hiring for roles that indicate a problem you solve
✅ What their team has published or commented on recently
✅ How their competitors are positioning
✅ What stage of growth the company is actually in based on headcount trajectory

Each of those is a data enrichment step. Each one requires a different source. And the orchestration between them matters more than any individual data point.

I build this as a Clay table with 15+ columns before a single email gets written. The enrichment waterfall runs through Apollo, LinkedIn, company site scraping, and job board analysis. By the time the sequence fires, each prospect has a research brief attached.

The email copy itself is almost trivial at that point. When you know what someone actually cares about, writing a relevant message is easy.

Stop hiring better copywriters for outbound. Start building better data pipelines.

Start with technographic signals. What tech do they actually run? Not what's on their marketing site. What's in their job postings and integration pages.

Add problem signals. Hiring patterns reveal priorities. Three open data engineering roles means they have a data problem, and they know it.

Layer in timing signals. Where are they in their buying cycle? A company evaluating vendors is a fundamentally different conversation than one locked into a 2-year contract.

My recommendation: build your enrichment as a pipeline, not a one-shot lookup. Each layer should have its own data source, its own refresh cadence, and its own quality check. The compound effect of multiple reliable signals is dramatically more valuable than one detailed enrichment that might be stale.

The teams getting the best reply rates aren't writing better emails. They're writing the right emails because their data tells them exactly what to say.

Everyone's optimizing copy. Subject line A/B tests, opening hook variations, CTA experiments. And none of it moves the needle much.

posts.title: Stop buying more tools
posts.hook: Your GTM stack doesn't need another tool. It needs the 6 tools you already have to actually talk to each other.
posts.body:
Your GTM stack doesn't need another tool. It needs the 6 tools you already have to actually talk to each other.

I audit GTM stacks regularly. The pattern is always the same.

CRM has 40% of contacts missing key fields. Enrichment tool runs but nobody mapped the output back to the CRM. Sequencing tool pulls from a static list that's 3 months stale. Analytics dashboard shows vanity metrics nobody acts on.

Then someone suggests buying a new tool to fix the gap.

The gap isn't a missing tool. It's missing plumbing.

When I rebuilt my own stack, I spent zero dollars on new tools for the first month. All I did was connect what I had:

➡️ Clay enrichment output writes directly to Attio via webhook
➡️ Attio stage changes trigger Lemlist sequence enrollment
➡️ PostHog events fire when prospects hit the website
➡️ Daily cron reconciles everything and flags data decay

The result wasn't more data. It was data that actually flowed. Prospects moved through the pipeline without manual handoffs. Nothing sat in a spreadsheet waiting for someone to copy-paste it somewhere.

Before you evaluate another vendor, map your current data flow. Actually draw it out. You'll find 3-4 broken connections that no new purchase will fix.

The most expensive tool in your stack is the one nobody connected properly.
posts.cta: What's the most broken connection in your current GTM stack? The one where data dies between two systems.

remixes.label: integration-audit-framework
remixes.hook: I audited 12 GTM stacks last year. Every single one had the same problem. Not missing tools. Missing connections between the tools they already owned.
remixes.body:
I audited 12 GTM stacks last year. Every single one had the same problem. Not missing tools. Missing connections between the tools they already owned.

The pattern is so consistent it's almost a law. Company buys CRM. Buys enrichment tool. Buys sequencing tool. Buys analytics platform. Each tool works fine in isolation. Together they form a data graveyard.

Here's what the audit usually reveals:

The CRM has 30-50% of contacts missing key fields. Not because nobody entered them. Because the enrichment tool writes to a custom field that no report or workflow actually references.

The sequencing tool pulls from a static list. That list was created 3 months ago. It doesn't reflect current enrichment data, recent disqualifications, or pipeline stage changes.

The analytics dashboard tracks opens and clicks. Nobody mapped those engagement signals back to pipeline progression. So the data exists but informs nothing.

The fix isn't buying a new tool. It's mapping every data handoff between your existing tools and asking: does this connection actually work? Is the data fresh? Does anything downstream use it?

I usually find 3-5 broken connections in every stack I audit. Fixing them costs nothing in software. It costs time in understanding how data flows through your specific system.

The ROI on fixing broken connections almost always exceeds the ROI on adding new tools. You already paid for the data. You're just not using it.
remixes.cta: Try this: trace one lead from first touch to closed-won through every tool in your stack. Where does the data break or go stale? That's your highest-leverage fix.

remixes.label: tool-buying-addiction
remixes.hook: GTM teams buy tools like they're collecting Pokemon. 12 platforms, 4 data sources, 3 analytics dashboards. And a pipeline that still runs on spreadsheets and Slack messages.
remixes.body:
GTM teams buy tools like they're collecting Pokemon. 12 platforms, 4 data sources, 3 analytics dashboards. And a pipeline that still runs on spreadsheets and Slack messages.

I've seen this movie enough times to recognize the plot.

Act 1: Team identifies a gap. Lead scoring is manual. Enrichment is inconsistent. Attribution is a guess. Solution: buy a tool.

Act 2: Tool gets implemented. Partially. The core features work. The integrations are "phase 2." Phase 2 never happens because a new gap appears and a new tool gets purchased.

Act 3: Two years later, the stack has 12 tools, costs six figures annually, and the team still exports CSVs to get a complete view of their pipeline.

The problem isn't the tools. Every one of them probably does what it promised. The problem is that nobody owns the connections between them. There's no integration engineer. There's no data flow diagram. There's no automated check that says "this enrichment data actually made it into the CRM record."

I build GTM systems for a living. The most impactful work I do is almost never adding new tools. It's drawing lines between existing ones. Building the API calls, the webhooks, the sync jobs that make data flow instead of stagnate.

Before you add tool number 13, map the data flow between the 12 you already have. I guarantee you'll find at least three connections that are either broken or never existed.
remixes.cta: What's the last tool you bought that actually integrated fully with your existing stack? Not just SSO. Actually moving data where it needs to go.

tones.advisor:
Your GTM stack doesn't need another tool. It needs the 6 tools you already have to actually talk to each other.

Here's the diagnostic I'd run on any GTM stack. I audit these regularly and the pattern is remarkably consistent.

First, check your CRM data completeness. Most have 30-50% of contacts missing key fields. The enrichment tool is running, but the output isn't mapped to the fields your workflows actually reference.

Second, check your list freshness. If your sequencing tool pulls from static lists, check when those lists were last updated. Stale lists mean you're reaching out to disqualified prospects and missing new ones.

Third, check your feedback loops. Are engagement signals from your outreach tool flowing back to your CRM? Are website visits updating lead scores? If these connections don't exist, your analytics are decorative.

My recommendation: before evaluating any new purchase, map every data handoff in your current stack. Draw it on a whiteboard. For each arrow between tools, verify the data is actually flowing, it's fresh, and something downstream uses it.

I typically find 3-5 broken connections in every stack audit. Fixing them requires zero software spend. Just engineering time to build the sync jobs, webhooks, or API calls that should have been built during implementation.

The highest ROI in most GTM operations isn't a new tool. It's making your existing tools work together.
tones.provocateur:
Your GTM stack doesn't need another tool. It needs the 6 tools you already have to actually talk to each other.

But you're going to buy another tool anyway. Because that's easier than admitting the last three purchases were never properly implemented.

I audit GTM stacks regularly. The pattern is depressingly consistent. CRM with 40% missing fields. Enrichment tool running into a void. Sequencing tool pulling from lists so stale they might as well be from 2024. Analytics dashboards showing metrics nobody acts on.

Every single one of these problems is a connection problem, not a capability problem. The tools work. The plumbing doesn't.

Here's why nobody fixes it. Buying a new tool feels like progress. It has a kickoff call and an implementation timeline and a dashboard you can show in the quarterly review. Fixing a broken API connection between your enrichment tool and your CRM? That has none of that visibility. It's invisible infrastructure work that nobody gets promoted for.

So the stack keeps growing. The data keeps fragmenting. And someone exports a CSV every Monday morning to build the report that three different analytics tools should be generating automatically.

Stop buying. Start connecting. The ROI on fixing what you already have almost always beats the ROI on adding something new.

posts.title: Career pivot pattern
posts.hook: Noticed a pattern across every successful career pivot into tech I've seen this year. None of them started with "I learned to code."
posts.body:
Noticed a pattern across every successful career pivot into tech I've seen this year. None of them started with "I learned to code."

They started with "I had a problem I couldn't solve with existing tools."

Daniel Vassallo left Amazon to ship small products. KP went from marketing to indie dev. Arvid Kahl built FeedbackPanda from a real classroom pain point.

Every one of them had domain knowledge first. The technical skills came second, in service of a problem they already understood deeply.

I see people on LinkedIn asking "should I learn Python or JavaScript first?" Wrong question. The question is: what process at your current job is so broken that you'd spend your weekend building something better?

That's your pivot vehicle.

Mine was outbound sales. I knew the workflow was broken because I lived in it every day. 200 emails, manual research, copy-pasting between 4 tabs. When I finally learned enough code to automate even one piece of it, I couldn't stop.

The code was never the point. The code was the lever.

If you're thinking about pivoting into building, don't start with a tutorial. Start with your biggest daily frustration. Build the smallest thing that makes it 20% less painful. Ship it. Then build the next piece.

The pivot happens when you realize you've been building for 6 months and forgot to update your LinkedIn title.
posts.cta: What was the frustration that pulled you into building? Not the aspiration. The actual pain point.

He advocates for 'small bets' – quick ships with low risk – and building in public to validate ideas. Post includes revenue shares and mindset shifts for sustainable solo building.

remixes.label: frustration-first-framework
remixes.hook: The best developers I know didn't start by picking a language. They started by being annoyed.
remixes.body:
The best developers I know didn't start by picking a language. They started by being annoyed.

Annoyed that their spreadsheet couldn't handle the workflow. Annoyed that the tool they were paying $200/month for solved 60% of the problem. Annoyed that nobody built the obvious thing.

So they built it themselves.

I keep seeing this in the indie builder space. The people who stick with it, who actually ship and find customers, almost never started from "I want to be a developer." They started from "this process is broken and I understand exactly why."

Domain expertise is the moat that tutorials can't teach. You can learn React in 3 months. You can't learn 8 years of understanding how freight logistics actually work in 3 months.

That's why career pivoters often outperform CS grads in certain product categories. They lived the problem. They know which pain points customers will pay to eliminate because they tried to eliminate them manually for years.

The code is just the last mile. The insight came first.
remixes.cta: What's a problem you understand deeply from your non-tech career that most developers would never even think about?

remixes.label: anti-bootcamp-take
remixes.hook: Bootcamps teach you to code. They don't teach you what to build. That's the gap nobody talks about.
remixes.body:
Bootcamps teach you to code. They don't teach you what to build. That's the gap nobody talks about.

I've watched dozens of bootcamp grads struggle to find traction. Not because they lack skills. Because they lack context. They can build a CRUD app but they don't know which CRUD app someone would pay for.

Meanwhile, the former logistics manager who taught herself Python to automate her own reporting? She shipped a paid tool in 4 months. Not because she's a better coder. Because she spent years sitting inside a problem that thousands of other logistics managers also have.

Daniel Vassallo talks about this. Small bets work when you have information asymmetry. When you know something about a market that most builders don't.

The pivot pattern that works: domain expertise first, technical skills second. Use your years in healthcare, finance, education, whatever it was. That knowledge compounds differently when you can also write code.

Stop thinking about "breaking into tech." Think about bringing tech into the industry you already understand.
remixes.cta: If you pivoted into building, was it your industry knowledge or your coding skills that gave you the early edge?

posts.title: Agent orchestration trap
posts.hook: Spent last week evaluating 4 different AI agent orchestration frameworks. Built something better in 200 lines of TypeScript.
posts.body:
Spent last week evaluating 4 different AI agent orchestration frameworks. Built something better in 200 lines of TypeScript.

The agent framework space right now feels like JavaScript frameworks circa 2016. New one every week. Each solves a slightly different problem. Each adds abstractions you probably don't need yet.

I tried CrewAI, Semantic Kernel, LangChain agents, and a custom setup. For my use case (GTM pipeline automation), every framework added complexity without adding capability.

What actually worked:

🔧 Claude Code subagents for parallel research tasks
🔧 Simple cron jobs that run enrichment scripts
🔧 Webhook chains between tools (no middleware)
🔧 SQLite for state management instead of a vector DB

Total infrastructure cost: $0 beyond API calls.

The frameworks are optimizing for flexibility I don't need. They assume you're building a general-purpose agent platform. I'm building a specific pipeline that does specific things on a schedule.

Most production agent deployments I've seen follow this same pattern. The winning architecture is boring. Cron + scripts + API calls + a simple database. Not a framework with 47 configuration options.

If your agent system needs a framework to explain how it works, it might be too complex.

Build the simplest thing that solves your actual problem. Add abstraction only when the simple thing breaks.
posts.cta: Anyone else finding that the "boring" architecture outperforms the frameworks? What's your production agent stack actually look like?

remixes.label: premature-abstraction-trap
remixes.hook: Every AI agent framework I evaluated last week solved problems I don't have yet. So I stopped evaluating and started building.
remixes.body:
Every AI agent framework I evaluated last week solved problems I don't have yet. So I stopped evaluating and started building.

CrewAI, Semantic Kernel, LangChain agents. Each one adds a layer of abstraction between you and the actual API calls. Role definitions, memory systems, tool registries, execution graphs.

All of that matters at scale. None of it matters when you're trying to get three API calls to happen in sequence with error handling.

I wrote 200 lines of TypeScript. A simple loop. Call the model, parse the response, execute the tool, feed results back. No framework. No dependency. Full control over retry logic and token costs.

It's been running in production for a week with zero issues.

The JavaScript framework wars of 2016 taught us this lesson already. The ecosystem explodes with options, everyone picks sides, and then the dust settles and the boring solution wins. React stuck around not because it was the most innovative. It stuck around because it was predictable.

Agent frameworks will consolidate the same way. In the meantime, you probably don't need one. You need a clear understanding of your orchestration requirements and a willingness to write the glue code yourself.
remixes.cta: What's the simplest agent architecture you've shipped to production? Genuinely curious what the minimal viable orchestration looks like for different use cases.

remixes.label: build-vs-buy-reality
remixes.hook: The agent framework you're evaluating this week will probably be deprecated by the time you finish integrating it.
remixes.body:
The agent framework you're evaluating this week will probably be deprecated by the time you finish integrating it.

That's not pessimism. That's the current rate of change.

I spent last week going deep on four orchestration frameworks. Each one had breaking changes in the last 60 days. Each one had GitHub issues from production users hitting edge cases the docs didn't cover. Each one assumed a set of architectural patterns that may or may not match your actual needs.

So I built my own. 200 lines. TypeScript. A loop that calls the model, parses structured output, executes tools, handles errors, and feeds results back.

The wild thing? It outperformed every framework I tested. Not because I'm a better engineer than those framework authors. Because my solution solves exactly my problem with zero overhead from solving everyone else's problem too.

Frameworks optimize for generality. Your production system optimizes for your specific workflow. Those goals are in tension.

The right time to adopt an agent framework is when your custom solution hits a complexity ceiling you can't manage. That ceiling is much higher than most people think. For most use cases today, you're better off with boring code you fully understand.

posts.title: Content that converts vs performs
posts.hook: My highest-performing LinkedIn post got 15K impressions. Generated zero pipeline. My lowest-performing post got 800 views. Generated 3 qualified conversations.
posts.body:
My highest-performing LinkedIn post got 15K impressions. Generated zero pipeline. My lowest-performing post got 800 views. Generated 3 qualified conversations.

The 15K post was a spicy take about sales tools. Broad audience. Lots of reactions. Nobody with budget cared.

The 800-view post was a detailed breakdown of how I connected Clay enrichment to CRM automation for a specific ICP. Tiny audience. But every person who read it was either building that exact workflow or hiring someone who could.

This is the content strategy gap most founders miss. They optimize for impressions because that's what the platform rewards visually. But pipeline comes from specificity, not reach.

The content that converts does three things:

✅ Describes a problem only your ICP has
✅ Shows enough of the solution that they know you actually built it
✅ Leaves one clear gap that requires a conversation to close

Generic "thought leadership" attracts generic audiences. Technical walkthroughs attract buyers.

I track this now. Every post gets tagged in PostHog. Which ones drive site visits. Which site visits become conversations. The correlation between impressions and pipeline is basically zero.

The correlation between specificity and pipeline is almost 1:1.

Write for the 50 people who have the exact problem you solve. Ignore the 15K who just want to hit the like button.
posts.cta: What's the ratio of your highest-impression content to your highest-pipeline content? Bet they're different posts.

inspired_by.author: Amy Volas
inspired_by.summary: Outlines a comprehensive content strategy for revenue operations, focusing on creating buyer-centric content that supports the full sales funnel. Details how sales ops can leverage content to shorten cycles and boost conversions. Includes metrics for measuring content ROI.

remixes.label: vanity-metrics-autopsy
remixes.hook: I tracked every LinkedIn post I published for 6 months against actual revenue. The correlation between impressions and pipeline was negative.
remixes.body:
I tracked every LinkedIn post I published for 6 months against actual revenue. The correlation between impressions and pipeline was negative.

Not zero. Negative. The posts that performed best on the platform performed worst for business.

Here's why that makes sense once you think about it.

Broad, spicy takes attract a broad audience. People who like arguing. People who like hitting the reaction button. People who will never buy anything from you.

Narrow, technical breakdowns attract a narrow audience. People dealing with the specific problem you just solved. People who read the whole post. People who DM you asking "can you do this for us."

My 15K impression post was a hot take about sales tools. My 800-view post was a detailed walkthrough of connecting Clay enrichment to CRM automation for a specific ICP.

Guess which one generated three qualified conversations and a closed deal.

The algorithm rewards engagement. Your business rewards relevance. Those are different objectives and optimizing for one actively hurts the other.

Stop measuring your content by how many people saw it. Start measuring it by how many of the right people acted on it.
remixes.cta: Have you ever tracked content performance against actual pipeline? What surprised you about the results?

remixes.label: icp-content-playbook
remixes.hook: Writing content for everyone is writing content for no one. Here's the exact framework I use to write posts that generate pipeline instead of applause.
remixes.body:
Writing content for everyone is writing content for no one. Here's the exact framework I use to write posts that generate pipeline instead of applause.

Step 1: Pick one person. Not a persona. An actual human in your ICP. Someone you've talked to. Write the post as if you're explaining something to them over coffee.

Step 2: Include implementation details that only matter to someone with budget. "We connected Clay to HubSpot via webhook" means nothing to casual scrollers. It means everything to the RevOps lead who's been trying to do exactly that.

Step 3: End with a question that filters. Not "what do you think?" but "anyone else running Clay-to-CRM automation? What enrichment sources are you using?" That question only attracts people who are already deep in the problem.

My highest-performing post got 15K impressions. Zero pipeline. My lowest-performing post got 800 views but generated 3 qualified conversations.

The difference was specificity. The 15K post was broadly interesting. The 800-view post was specifically useful.

LinkedIn rewards reach. Your business rewards resonance. Choose which game you're playing.
remixes.cta: What's one post you wrote that bombed on metrics but actually drove business? Drop it below.

Your viral LinkedIn post is worthless. I can prove it with data.

15K impressions on my hottest take. Zero pipeline. Zero conversations that mattered. Zero revenue.

The content marketing industry has gaslit us into believing that impressions equal impact. They don't. Impressions measure how many people paused their scroll for half a second. Pipeline measures how many people picked up the phone.

You know who engages with broad, spicy takes? Other content creators. Engagement farmers. People who will never have budget for your services. You're performing for an audience of non-buyers and calling it strategy.

The post that actually works is the one that makes 95% of LinkedIn users scroll past. Because it's too specific. Too technical. Too niche. But the 5% who stop? They're your buyers. They recognize themselves in the details.

Stop chasing impressions. Start writing posts that only your ICP would care about. Your analytics will look worse. Your pipeline will look better. Pick one.

posts.title: Open source your GTM brain
posts.hook: Open-source AI models are catching up to closed ones. Same thing is about to happen in GTM.
posts.body:
Open-source AI models are catching up to closed ones. Same thing is about to happen in GTM.

Karpathy keeps advocating for open-source AI alternatives. The argument is simple. When infrastructure becomes commoditized, the advantage shifts to whoever uses it most creatively.

GTM is following the same arc.

Two years ago, having access to a premium data provider was a competitive advantage. Now everyone has Apollo. Everyone has Clay. Everyone has the same enrichment sources.

The advantage shifted. It's not about what data you can access. It's about what you build on top of it.

I run Qwen 2.5 locally for high-frequency tasks. Free. No API costs. It handles lead scoring, content drafts, data normalization. The expensive models (Claude Opus) handle the complex stuff. Strategy, voice-matched content, multi-step reasoning.

Tiered model architecture for GTM operations. Local models for volume, cloud models for judgment.

This is where solo operators and small teams win. You don't need enterprise contracts with every data vendor. You need a well-architected pipeline that combines cheap/free resources intelligently.

The playbook is the same as open-source AI. Commoditize the infrastructure layer. Win on the orchestration layer.

The teams still paying premium for basic enrichment are subsidizing features they'll never use. Build your own stack from open components. Keep the margin.
posts.cta: Running any local models in your GTM workflow? Curious what tasks people are offloading from paid APIs.

inspired_by.author: Andrej Karpathy
inspired_by.summary: Explores the infrastructure challenges in training and deploying large-scale AI models, advocating for open-source alternatives to proprietary cloud services. Details a new stack for efficient AI infra using tools like Ray and Kubernetes. Predicts automation in infra management will accelerate agent adoption.

remixes.label: commoditization-playbook
remixes.hook: Every GTM tool you're paying $500/month for will be free within 18 months. The question is what you do with that reality right now.
remixes.body:
Every GTM tool you're paying $500/month for will be free within 18 months. The question is what you do with that reality right now.

Open-source AI caught up to GPT-4 quality for most practical tasks. Llama, Mistral, Qwen. Running locally. Zero API costs. Good enough for 80% of workflows.

GTM tooling is following the same curve.

Enrichment data? Multiple free and open alternatives are emerging. Outbound sequencing? Open-source options exist. CRM intelligence? You can build it with a local model and your own data.

Two years ago, having access to ZoomInfo or Apollo was a competitive advantage. Now the data is commoditized. The advantage shifted from access to application.

Karpathy has been saying this about AI models. When the infrastructure becomes free, the value moves up the stack to whoever applies it most creatively.

The teams winning at GTM right now aren't the ones with the most expensive tool stack. They're the ones who understand their ICP deeply enough to use cheap tools effectively. A $0/month local model running enrichment overnight beats a $500/month API you use for batch jobs once a week.

The playbook: start replacing your most expensive, least differentiated tools with open-source alternatives. Reinvest the savings into the things that actually differentiate you.
remixes.cta: Which paid GTM tools have you replaced with open-source or local alternatives? What worked and what didn't?

remixes.label: diy-gtm-stack
remixes.hook: I run my entire GTM pipeline on a Mac Mini with local AI models. Monthly cost: $0 in compute. Here's the stack.
remixes.body:
I run my entire GTM pipeline on a Mac Mini with local AI models. Monthly cost: $0 in compute. Here's the stack.

Qwen 2.5 running locally via Ollama handles enrichment scoring, content categorization, and ICP matching. No API costs. Runs overnight. Processes hundreds of records while I sleep.

The thesis is simple. Most GTM intelligence tasks don't need GPT-4 level reasoning. They need pattern matching on structured data. Local models do that just fine.

I still use paid APIs for the tasks that genuinely require them. Complex content generation. Multi-step reasoning chains. Anything customer-facing where quality variance matters.

But the high-frequency, high-volume grunt work? That runs locally.

This mirrors what's happening in the broader AI infrastructure space. Karpathy's advocacy for open-source models isn't ideological. It's practical. When the base capability is free, you can afford to run it at frequencies and volumes that would bankrupt you on paid APIs.

The GTM teams that figure this out first will have a structural cost advantage. Not just saving money. Running analyses their competitors can't afford to run. Enriching data at intervals their competitors can't match. Testing hypotheses their competitors can't test because each API call costs money.

Open-source doesn't mean worse. It means unconstrained.
remixes.cta: What GTM tasks are you still running through paid APIs that a local model could handle? Curious where people are drawing that line.

posts.title: Layoffs create builders
posts.hook: Tech layoffs created more indie builders in 18 months than the last decade of "learn to code" campaigns.
posts.body:
Tech layoffs created more indie builders in 18 months than the last decade of "learn to code" campaigns.

Gergely Orosz has been tracking this. Founders and solo devs shipping products after getting laid off. Not because they dreamed of entrepreneurship. Because they realized depending on a single employer was the actual risk.

I left my corporate role in March. Not because of layoffs, but the timing wasn't a coincidence. The writing was on the wall. Teams shrinking. Budgets freezing. The people who survived were doing the work of three.

So I took everything I knew about GTM operations and built my own stack. One Mac Mini running cron jobs. Three websites. Full ABM pipeline. Content distribution system. Solo.

The first month was terrifying. No steady paycheck. No team to lean on. Just me and a terminal.

The second month, something shifted. Every hour I invested compounded. The systems I built kept working while I slept. The pipeline didn't need a manager's approval to improve.

I'm not romanticizing it. Some weeks are brutal. Cash flow is lumpy. The loneliness is real.

But I'd rather build systems I own than optimize systems I rent.

If you're on the fence, the gap between "thinking about building" and "actually building" is exactly one shipped project. Not a perfect one. A shipped one.
posts.cta: For those who made the jump, what was the project that made it real? Not the idea. The first thing you actually shipped.

inspired_by.author: Gergely Orosz
inspired_by.summary: Gergely reflects on founders and solo devs shipping products amid tech layoffs, highlighting success stories of pivots into indie hacking. He stresses building in public for feedback loops and shares data on bootstrapped successes versus VC paths. Provides actionable tips for career switchers into tech building.

remixes.label: necessity-beats-passion
remixes.hook: "Follow your passion" created dreamers. Getting laid off created shippers.
remixes.body:
"Follow your passion" created dreamers. Getting laid off created shippers.

There's a wave of indie builders right now who didn't choose entrepreneurship. Entrepreneurship chose them when their employer handed them a box and a severance check.

Gergely Orosz has been documenting this. Former FAANG engineers shipping real products within months of being let go. Not because they suddenly discovered a passion for startups. Because they discovered that a W-2 was a single point of failure.

The psychology matters here. When building your own thing is a hobby, you can procrastinate forever. When it's your only source of income in 6 months, you ship fast and cut scope ruthlessly.

Constraint drives execution. Always has.

I left my corporate role in March. Not from a layoff, but the timing aligned with the same realization. The safety of employment was an illusion. The salary was real but so was the dependency.

What I've noticed about post-layoff builders: they skip the "what should I build" phase faster than anyone. They already know. They spent years inside companies watching problems go unsolved. They know which internal tools were held together with duct tape. They know which workflows were needlessly manual.

The layoff just gave them permission to fix it.
remixes.cta: If you made the jump, voluntary or not, what was the first thing you actually shipped? Not the idea list. The thing that went live.

remixes.label: single-employer-risk
remixes.hook: We tell junior devs to diversify their stock portfolio but not their income. That's the real career risk nobody talks about.
remixes.body:
We tell junior devs to diversify their stock portfolio but not their income. That's the real career risk nobody talks about.

Tech layoffs hit 260,000+ people in 2023. Another wave in 2024. Each one of those people had 100% of their income concentrated in a single entity's decision to keep them.

Financial advisors would call that irresponsible concentration risk. But somehow when it's your career instead of your portfolio, we call it "stability."

The builders who came out of layoffs strongest weren't the ones with the best severance. They were the ones who had already started something on the side. A newsletter. A small SaaS. A consulting pipeline. Anything that meant their income didn't go from full to zero overnight.

Gergely Orosz has tracked this pattern. The post-layoff founders who shipped fastest had been noodling on ideas for months or years before the layoff hit. The layoff didn't create the builder. It removed the excuse.

I left my corporate role in March. Built my own GTM engineering practice. The transition was smoother than it should have been because I'd been building in public for months before making the jump.

The lesson isn't "quit your job." The lesson is "build something yours while you still have a paycheck." The best time to diversify your income is before you need to.
